title: Piers Plowman (A, B, and C versions), Richard the Redeless, and The Crowned King
summary: The repository XML copies U-M CME item AJT8124.0001.001, which encodes Skeat's 1867 A, 1869 B, and 1873 C/appended-poems carriers. Piers Plowman is one work with A/B/C transmission streams; Richard the Redeless and The Crowned King are separate anonymous works. Skeat's A, B, and C texts use composite manuscript and print-intermediary paths, including Wright before B.15.17, Whitaker before HM 137, and three separately collated Crowley impressions. Historical witness R is one textual witness now split between Rawlinson Poetry 38 and Lansdowne 398. Forty-eight exact ignored cache objects are mapped once each, including complete codices or provider sequences where obtainable and fourteen one-page LMeS private-study tile grids.

editorial_practices.description: Composite A construction: Vernon through XI.180, R.3.14 XI.181-303, Rawlinson Poetry 137 Passus XII, plus scoped collations.
Laud Misc. 581 base with manuscript and separately modeled print intermediaries, including Wright and each Crowley impression.
Whitaker/HM 137 base plus scoped C collations; Bodley 851 was selected but not fully collated.
The electronic carrier encodes Skeat's A/B/C texts and the two appended political poems but omits much of the printed front/back matter.

evidence.citation: General Corpus pinned ajt8124 XML and structural audit
evidence.summary: The XML contains exactly three intellectual works; Piers is one work with A/B/C streams. It has 18,184 serialized L elements, of which nine are NOTE1-nested quotations, leaving 18,175 direct intellectual verse lines. It also has 171 direct LG groups and 63 sections.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan, AJT8124.0001.001 item information and contents
evidence.summary: The exact item supplies the inherited title, Langland carrier attribution, publication span 1867-85, EETS context, public-domain/No Copyright-US statement, and a TOC separating A, B, C, Richard the Redeless, and The Crowned King.

evidence.summary: Skeat identifies Laud Misc. 581 as base and distinguishes historical witness R, Wright, the three Crowley impressions, and the remaining B collations.

evidence.citation: Walter W. Skeat, The Vision of William Concerning Piers the Plowman, Part IV, section 1 (1877)
evidence.summary: Skeat describes the Whitaker/Phillipps base, the C collations and their scopes, and the Ll.4.14 and Douce 95 appended poems.
evidence.locator: C-text manuscript descriptions and collation account

evidence.citation: Walter W. Skeat, The Vision of William Concerning Piers the Plowman, Part IV, section 1 (1877)
evidence.summary: The scan establishes Part IV.i as EETS OS 67 (1877) and supplies the critical apparatus used to reconstruct the edition/witness chains.

evidence.citation: Walter W. Skeat, The Vision of William Concerning Piers the Plowman, Part IV, section 2
evidence.summary: The delivered scan's title page is dated 1885 and identifies EETS OS 81, while the Internet Archive identifier and filename label it 1884. Its preface and indexes support the work boundaries and edition history.
evidence.locator: title page, general preface, manuscript notes, and indexes

evidence.citation: Thomas Wright, ed., The Vision and Creed of Piers Ploughman, 2 vols. (1842)
evidence.summary: The two-volume edition is the print intermediary through which Skeat used B.15.17 and limited R.3.14 readings.
evidence.locator: volume 1 title, preface, and textual source discussion; volume 2 continuation
notes: The second volume is separately cached and fixity-recorded under access:wright-1842-volume-2-pdf.

evidence.citation: Skeat's account of John Whitaker's 1813 C-text edition
evidence.summary: Skeat states that the C base reaches him through Whitaker's transcription of the manuscript then designated Phillipps 8231; that manuscript is now Huntington HM 137, not HM 143.

evidence.citation: Piers Plowman Electronic Archive, Crowley's three 1550 impressions
evidence.summary: PPEA distinguishes Cr1, Cr2, and Cr3. Skeat collated the impressions separately; the evidence does not justify a print-to-print chain.

evidence.citation: James M. Dean, ed., Richard the Redeless and Mum and the Sothsegger (METS, 2000)
evidence.summary: The edition treats Richard the Redeless as anonymous, dates and contextualizes it around 1399, identifies Ll.4.14 ff. 107v-119v as its witness, and describes its alliterative political-satiric form.
The scholarly presentation gives Richard the Redeless circa 1399-1400 following Helen Barr and The Crowned King circa 1415, while listing their manuscripts and editions.

evidence.citation: Linguistic Geographies / Medieval Scribes selected-page image-rights statements
evidence.summary: Each of the fourteen inspected aspect pages identifies one selected manuscript page and says use beyond private study requires written permission from the holding library.
evidence.locator: Image Rights field; fourteen aspect pages individually checked

evidence.citation: U.S. Copyright Office, Circular 15A: Duration of Copyright
evidence.summary: The circular supports conservative U.S. term analysis and cautions that foreign/restored works and distinct modern layers require separate treatment.
The official service supplies high-quality digitization of special collections for academic, commercial, and personal use and accepts image orders when an item is not fully available online.

The official beta order route accepts manuscript shelfmarks and folio ranges, distinguishes reference and reproduction images, asks users to specify colour, and routes publication licensing separately.

evidence.citation: Cambridge University Library Image Supply and Reproduction
evidence.summary: The official service links its imaging form, permits multiple pages from up to five items, distinguishes private-study and publication requests, quotes before purchase, and makes supply subject to condition and permissions.

evidence.citation: Newnham College Library 2025-26 introduction and special-collections contact
evidence.summary: The current official guide directs enquiries to library@newn.cam.ac.uk. No dedicated manuscript-digitization order form was verified, so the route is recorded only as an enquiry.

evidence.citation: Senate House Library Reproductions and Digitisation Services
evidence.summary: The official page describes manuscript digitization and prices but currently states that no reproduction or digitization orders are available until at least September 2026; enquiries remain possible by email.

evidence.citation: University College Oxford, Library Special Collections
evidence.summary: The College states that its collection of more than two hundred manuscripts is housed in the Bodleian Library, supporting use of Bodleian Imaging Services for MS 45 while retaining University College ownership in notes.

review_notes: Exactly three intellectual items are represented; Piers counts once and its A/B/C children are version streams.
Direct intellectual extent is 18,175 verse lines. The 18,184 raw L count includes nine NOTE1-nested quotation lines and is retained only as an encoding metric.
Richard the Redeless and The Crowned King are modeled as anonymous; Skeat's historical Langland attribution is not a modern responsibility claim, and Langland is never assigned to The Crowned King.
All 48 local copies occur exactly once across access records: 33 target-present copies with exact work portions and 15 target-absent copies without work portions.
All fourteen LMeS ZIPs are one-page partial/private-study caches of the complete highest-resolution grid, not full manuscripts and not complete multi-resolution provider pyramids.

Rights cover all 73 entities, with fourteen additional access-layer records for mediated reproduction orders; underlying text, witnesses, editions, encodings, photographs, catalog expressions, and provider terms remain distinct.
No relation says that the repository artifact consulted research sources merely because this manifest research did.
